# Transcrição: 03-Ecommerce Example

- **Fonte:** 03-Ecommerce Example
- **Duração:** 16:30
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

In this video, I'm going to take you through a typical funnel strategy that we use for e-commerce. Now, it's important to note that when it comes to funnel strategies, you have to tailor it for every website, every eventuality, the products of different categories, the spend values, etc. So this is a typical funnel. And what I do is when I start working with an e-commerce site, I use this as a template to then determine whether it's the right approach, whether we need to tweak things slightly, etc. But one of the key things here is we're following a structured process of four key layers of our customers, so from cold and warm to hot and also existing customers, and then really thinking about the right campaigns for these customers and the targeting. And then all the way through to the kind of optimization and the creative as well. And I want to talk through the four layers in a bit more detail of how we would approach this for an e-commerce account. So at the top layer, you have your cold audience targeting. The three key campaigns that I'm playing with here are page post engagement, video views, and website conversion. I see a lot of e-commerce Facebook marketers using website conversion, maybe overlooking page post engagement, video views, etc. That for me is a big failure because the audiences that you're playing with, whether they're in the hundreds of thousands or tens of thousands or millions, are made up of multiple different people who are in different buying states and buying cycles. So you may have an audience of, you know, one million and you create an objective for page post engagement. There are probably buyers in there or people that can be converted into buyers. That Facebook otherwise didn't realize are ready to take a conversion to buy, for example. And also I use this as part of my testing strategy. So if you've been through the graduation framework, page post engagement is an important part of that as well. One thing to note is page post engagement and the video views objectives are very similar in the way they operate. So at the core, they're both after engagement. What video views offers you are additional tools to make the video creative a lot more optimized for that objective as well. And so I usually run these three together in cold audience targeting. And this is where I'm bringing in broad interests, partner character categories, look-alikes, behaviors, etc. Running website conversion campaigns to try and generate sales to scale up to bring traffic into the site. And using page post engagement for testing in video views is a combination of both. So it really depends on the type of product and the average order value that we're working with. But, you know, if I'm looking for a cold audience and I want to sift out the right people and I don't really have a strong avatar, then a video view campaign is a great way of doing that.

## [03:00 - 06:00]

Where you can actually run a video and you can retarget people based on 10 second view or 50% or 75% viewed and create a much warmer audience that we can then target off the back of that. So going down to that level two, that's one of the examples of an ad set that I run there. So against a website traffic, website conversion or a traffic objective, I might run a video view custom audience campaign where I'm retargeting people that have viewed a percentage of the video. Or it may be fans that have not purchased or emails I've grabbed off Facebook or from the website of people that have not purchased. Well, why am I using traffic at this level? The traffic objective is looking at your audience or your pot of data and just finding people who are likely to click on the ad. Now, the difference between that and website conversion is you can optimize that activity further down your funnel. So typically for e-commerce, you have different stages that you'd set pixel events for. You might have one for your product page, which generally people use as your content. One for ad to basket, one for your visited cart, initiate payment, payment page and confirmation page. And so if you have a website conversion campaign, you can optimize for those different objectives and try and get Facebook to find people who are likely to land on one of those events. What the traffic objective does is it goes a few levels above that and actually just gets people that are likely to click off the ad or click on the ad. And what that does is it puts less of an onus on you to get conversions in to make the algorithm work. So recently Facebook upgraded their requirement for conversions in a given week from 25 to 50, which has made it a lot more harder for small spenders, small stores to actually start to generate enough data for website conversion to work really well. And then you have to play with different layers. So you might have a website conversion campaign that's going for purchase, but there's just not enough events firing. So Facebook can't find the right people. This is prevalent especially for when you're starting out an e-commerce store and when you don't have much spend going in. What the traffic campaign allows you to do, especially for a warm audience, and you know, bear in mind if they are already fans, if they're emails that are on your list and they haven't purchased, get them onto your website. And this is where sometimes a traffic campaign can do a good job. Alternatively, you might want to use the website conversion campaign with a landing page objective or optimize for view content, for example. And what you want to do at the warm stages is just get them back to the site. So there's different creators you can play around with. And generally at this stage, I would start to kind of play around with a few more placements. So at the cold level, generally I'm testing with Facebook, may start to introduce Instagram and see if we can actually bring some traffic in. You might want to consider splitting a placement depending on how you're messaging your proposition.

## [06:00 - 09:06]

If you're starting out, I would generally group the placements together. But as you start to scale out and we'll go through this in the scaling video, you can start splitting those placements out and seeing if you're getting better performance with pushing budgets into different placements as well. Then the third layer down is retargeting. Now with this, there's three key objectives again that I'm using. Website conversion, product catalog and traffic again. From testing with an e-commerce, I often see that when you run website conversion and traffic together, especially when you're not running product catalog, you can get some really good performance. They actually blended, they give a good blended performance. What happens is with website conversion, especially in retargeting when you're trying to get people back to purchase, Facebook may or may not get it right. So it may find the right people that read it by. Sometimes it doesn't. But finding that with a traffic campaign allows you to widen that pot. So it allows the website conversion campaign to go off and try and find the best audiences that are likely to convert and traffic shores up the rest of those people in that audience that may convert. And it's something to test and to test those running together. Some people just like to run traffic because at this stage, you know, you might have a smaller pot of hundreds or thousands of people who are highly likely to buy. And therefore you just want the traffic campaign objectives to just bring them back to your site. If you have a product catalog, you know, nine times out of 10, it will definitely perform better than just running normal ads. Being able to set up your catalog in the background with all your products and actually once someone visits your product page, getting that those products back into their newsfeed with similar products generally outperforms anything else as well. I push this into people that have viewed a product or added to cart and that generally works well, but also looking at the timings as well. So you can you have to play around with the targets and rules. But as a general preference, I will start retarget people that visit the site but not product page for seven days, people that have viewed a product page but not added to cart in seven days. And also the kind of add to cart sequence of seven to 14 to 28 days. And these timings need to be tested for every e-commerce store. So I would take this rough setup or this draft setup, test it and see what kind of reaction I'm getting. Now when you're starting off, you probably don't have enough volume of people to separate your add to cart into seven days, 14 days, 28 days, in which case group it together and just have one ad set which is people that have added to cart in the last 28 days. And let Facebook optimise to get the right people into back into your website as well. And also when it comes to optimisation, you can play around with the kind of one day click, seven day click. Generally, if it's a low ticket value item, then the one day click, if you get in good volume of sales coming through is better. So it gets Facebook to just focus on the people that are likely to convert within one day and have converted over the last 24 hours.

## [09:06 - 12:06]

The seven day post click I find works better when you know that the consideration process takes a bit longer. So the advantage of that is let's say you have an item that's selling at $200 and you know that it takes five days on average for people to convert. You want more data for Facebook to take into account. So that's where you'd start to use bigger optimisation periods as well. And when it comes to creative, it's about testing which formats work. Definitely go for dynamic product ads for retargeting if you can. Then the fourth layer down is for existing customers. And this is part of the kind of process which has been left out by a lot of people that run e-commerce stores is with Facebook's cost per thousand impressions increasing over the last few years. And probably going to continue to increase as more competitors come on board and as fewer and fewer placements are made available. Monetising your existing customers is crucial. So I would highly recommend that you focus on your lifetime value as much as you do on cost per acquisition and return on ad spend. So for example, let's say that you break even on that first purchase. Let's say your margin is 50% on the product and your ad spend is whatever it is. If you make 150% back in terms of return on ad spend and you can pay off your ad spend and you can also pay off your cost of goods sold. Then for me, that is a good outcome if you've got a strong post purchase sequence set up. So the post purchase sequence could be immediately after the sale on your website. So you deliver them a one time offer. Maybe it's multiple one time offers. Maybe it's an email sequence that goes on afterwards, but also consider Facebook ads as well. So for those that have purchased, can we retarget them and bring them back in later on down the line? There is actually a study that's been applied on buying behaviour and actually where the peak moments are to sell. So the first point is after the purchase something for you from you, they're on a natural high. And so that's a great time to actually hit them up with a new offer. Also after they've received their product, they're on another high as well. So shipping could take days, it could take weeks depending on what type of product is hitting them at the point that they receive the product and actually use it as another high point as well. And then maybe then thinking about what the product cycle is. So if for example, let's say you're selling a perishable, whether it's shampoo or something like that, and generally you know that it takes six weeks for a typical customer to properly use that item, then start serving those ads up six weeks after the first purchase, maybe even five, seven weeks, test this out, and try to get them into the resale. So you'd imagine to have an email sequence mirroring that as well,

## [12:06 - 15:06]

but Facebook is another way to actually bring them back with a much lower cost proposition as well. Also with Facebook, one of the other opportunities is to use dynamic product ads for those existing customers as well. And what that allows you to do is you can create a sub-catalog of your main catalogue. And let's say for example you're running multiple categories, let's take Jewelry for example, and the customers purchase to watch, then the sub-category might be bracelets or chains or necklaces or something like that. And you can start to run retargeting ads to existing customers knowing that they purchase one particular category to try and get them to purchase a different category as well. And so they're just different techniques you can use to build up your account setup. So in terms of an example of how this might look in terms of a campaign, this is a campaign which we're running right now actually, which breaks out those different layers. So you can see the layer one, two, three, and four, and we've labeled it as cold, warm, hot, and existing. And what I've done here is to also put the objectives and also the country targeting here. There's different ways of setting this up. You can bundle all your countries together. My own preference is to keep the country at the campaign level. Sometimes depending on if it's a general store or a multi-category store, I'll also have the category at campaign level as well. So looking at how that looks within a typical campaign, so this is a campaign we've been running recently. And you can see that the structure is here that we've been applying. But one of the key things to point out is when I set up the structure, I'm not expecting to use every single campaign and objective all at once. I'm testing, I'm learning, I want to see how customers are reacting, how the accounts are performing, etc., etc. Before, I start to dive really deep in and playing around with all the different objectives. But I prefer to have it set up from the start. So when I need to use something, it's there. One of the things just to point out and a common question I get is, how much should we put into cold audience targeting versus testing versus retargeting? This is a typical e-commerce account. And for example here, I think around 84%, 85% of our targeting is going into our cold website conversion targeting. Here, I've got around, I don't know, 5% I think of the spend going into testing. And my recommendation is always to have 5% to 10% in your testing. It really depends on where you are with growth and where you are with testing. If your growth is fairly stable, then your testing might fluctuate between 5% and 10%. If you're scaling, then it may drop down. But in this example, we've got 5% here. And here for retargeting, we have about 10%, 11% going into retargeting. And this is the example where I gave of running the traffic and website conversion campaigns together.

## [15:07 - 16:30]

And this is, we've seen some kind of fluctuating results here. So with the cost per acquisition overall target of $35 at this stage, we've seen that website conversion sometimes brings in a lower cost per acquisition. Sometimes it's traffic, but running them blended actually gives us the best results. And that's something worth testing for your e-com setup as well. And see if whether running the objectives together works out as well. And going back into the setup, just to kind of reflect back, this is kind of the setup that I've taken, which is taking our vanilla approach to account setup and just applying it into the account. So you can see this is the way it relates down all the way through hot and through to existing customers. This is a typical account setup. My recommendation would be to use something similar as your kind of start base and to test and iterate as you go along as you start to understand what works on your account and what doesn't. There is no set structure that this is the absolute best way of setting up your e-commerce store. It all comes back to getting the basics in place, making sure that for whichever level that you're currently targeting, you are pushing the right creatives or optimizations in place and funneling people down through each part of the funnel and then closing that sale out.
